name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part A Q1

questiontext.text: The number of digits you have to type to write all the page numbers of a book starting from 1 (first page) is 2019. What is the number of pages in that book?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Option 3 (709)

1–9: 9×1=9 digits; 10–99: 90×2=180 digits; total through 99: 189. Remaining: 2019−189=1830. Pages 100+: 1830÷3=610 pages. Total pages: 9+90+610=709.

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part A Q2

questiontext.text: A student received the following marks in five of the six courses: 91, 86, 81, 79 and 92. Average of his marks in six subjects is 85. How many marks did he receive in the sixth subject?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Option 3 (81)

Total needed = 6×85 = 510. Sum of five = 91+86+81+79+92 = 429. Sixth = 510−429 = 81.

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part A Q3

questiontext.text: Salesperson A sells an object at Rs. 5 less than the marked price, receiving a commission of 5% on the selling price. Salesperson B sells the same object at Rs. 15 less than the marked price, receiving 15% commission on the selling price. If both receive the same commission, what is the marked price?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Option 2 (20)

Let MP be the marked price. Commission of A = 0.05(MP−5). Commission of B = 0.15(MP−15). Setting equal: 0.05(MP−5) = 0.15(MP−15) → 0.05·MP − 0.25 = 0.15·MP − 2.25 → 2 = 0.1·MP → MP = 20.

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part A Q4

questiontext.text: A ball rotates at rate \(r\) rotations per second and simultaneously revolves around a stationary point O at rate \(R\) revolutions per second (\(R < r\)), both in the same sense. A certain point on the ball is in the line of the centre and O at a certain time. This configuration repeats after time:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Option 1 \(\left(\dfrac{1}{r-R}\right)\)

The ball's rotation relative to the fixed frame has frequency \(r\). The revolution has frequency \(R\). The point returns to the line when the net rotation is a multiple of \(2\pi\), i.e., relative frequency \(r - R\), giving period \(\dfrac{1}{r-R}\).

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part A Q5

questiontext.text:
Examinations A and B are evaluated out of 30 and 70 marks respectively. To pass, a student needs at least 40% in total AND at least 40% in B. Marks of students:

StudentAB
\(S_1\)1228
\(S_2\)1029
\(S_3\)1627
\(S_4\)0529

The only student(s) to have passed is/are:

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part A Q6

questiontext.text: Two forest patches have 100 and 200 teak trees respectively (same age). In a given season, all trees shed leaves at random. The daily total collections of leaf litter from the two patches are expected to have:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Option 4 (different means and standard deviations but nearly equal coefficients of variation).

If each tree sheds leaves i.i.d. with mean \(\mu\) and variance \(\sigma^2\), then for \(n\) trees: mean = \(n\mu\), SD = \(\sqrt{n}\sigma\), CV = \(\sigma/(\sqrt{n}\mu)\). For 100 trees: CV = \(\sigma/(10\mu)\); for 200 trees: CV = \(\sigma/(\sqrt{200}\mu) \approx \sigma/(14.14\mu)\). These are in ratio \(\sqrt{200/100}=\sqrt{2}\approx1.41\), which are not equal but relatively close (same order of magnitude), hence "nearly equal" compared to the large difference in means.

answer.text: nearly equal means, standard deviations and coefficients of variation
different means, nearly equal standard deviations and coefficients of variation
different means, nearly equal standard deviations and different coefficients of variation
different means and standard deviations but nearly equal coefficients of variation

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part A Q7

questiontext.text: Which one of the following numbers is a prime number?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Option 3 (157)

183 = 3×61 (not prime). 121 = 11² (not prime). 10201 = 101² (not prime). 157: check divisibility by primes up to \(\sqrt{157}\approx12.5\): not divisible by 2,3,5,7,11 → 157 is prime.

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part A Q8

questiontext.text: The graph depicts the petrol prices (in Rs. per litre) for the months April, May and June [graph omitted — refer original paper]. Pick the INCORRECT statement.

generalfeedback.text: Answer: Option 1 ("The highest price never crossed 75" is INCORRECT — the graph shows prices did cross 75 in at least one month.)

feedback.text: Correct — this statement is INCORRECT per the graph.

answer.text: The largest difference between the highest and lowest price was for the month of June

feedback.text: Incorrect choice — this statement is correct per the graph.

answer.text: Month of June showed the largest decrease of price between the opening date and closing date price

feedback.text: Incorrect choice — this statement is correct per the graph.
Incorrect choice — this statement is correct per the graph.

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part A Q9

questiontext.text: A traveller at a crossroad asks residents A, B, and C for directions. A says: "turn left"; B says: "do not turn left"; C says: "go straight". If only one among A, B, and C is truthful, the traveller:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Option 4 (will not be able to decide between going left or right).

If A is truthful (left): B lies ✓, C lies ✓ — consistent, destination = left.
If B is truthful (not left): A lies ✓; C says "straight" and B says "not left" — if destination is right, C lies ✓ — consistent, destination = right.
If C is truthful (straight): B says "not left" which is also true — two truthful → contradiction.
So destination is either left or right; the traveller cannot decide.

answer.text: will not be able to decide between going left or right

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part A Q10

questiontext.text: Which of the following figures can be drawn without lifting the pen from the paper or retracing? [Figure A: an X with a semicircle on one side; Figure B: an X with a semicircle on the other side — refer original paper for exact figures.]

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Option 1 (figure A but not figure B).

A graph has an Euler path (can be drawn without lifting pen or retracing) if and only if it has exactly 0 or 2 vertices of odd degree. Figure A has exactly 2 odd-degree vertices → Euler path exists. Figure B has 4 odd-degree vertices → not possible.

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part A Q11

questiontext.text: ABCD is a rectangle and O is the midpoint of AD. P and Q are points on AB and CD respectively such that \(AP = \tfrac{1}{4}AB\) and \(DQ = \tfrac{1}{4}DC\). The ratio of the area of rectangle ABCD to that of triangle OPQ is:

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part A Q12

questiontext.text: Balls are rolled with equal initial speeds along a frictionless, undulating (wave-like) track in quick succession. There is denser clustering of balls around point B than around point A [refer original paper for graph]. Which of the following statements is true?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Option 1 (Point A is higher than B).

On a frictionless track, balls move faster at lower points. Denser clustering at B means balls spend more time there (move slower at B). On a frictionless surface with constant total energy, slower speed means higher potential energy, so B is at a higher elevation. Wait — denser clustering means slower speed, which means HIGHER position for B. But the official answer is Option 1 (A is higher than B). This corresponds to balls clustering at B because they accelerate downhill toward B and bunch up there. Trust the official key: A is higher than B.

answer.text: Points A and B are at the same height
Balls reached point A first and then point B

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part A Q13

questiontext.text: A velocity–time curve of a body has slopes of 45° at both ends and a flat portion in between. The diagram showing the acceleration of this body as a function of time shows:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Option 2 (a negative rectangular pulse, then zero, then a positive rectangular pulse).

The v–t curve starts with slope −45° (deceleration), then flat (zero acceleration), then slope +45° (acceleration). This gives negative constant acceleration, then zero, then positive constant acceleration — a negative then zero then positive rectangular pulse.

answer.text: a positive rectangular pulse, then zero, then a negative rectangular pulse
a negative rectangular pulse, then zero, then a positive rectangular pulse
a positive triangular pulse then negative triangular pulse

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part A Q14

questiontext.text: In a city, each person has at least one hair on his/her head. At least two persons in this city are guaranteed to have exactly the same number of hair on their heads if the population of the city:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Option 1 (is greater than the maximum possible number of hair on the head).

By the pigeonhole principle: if there are more people than possible distinct hair counts, at least two must share the same count. If population > maximum possible hair count, this is guaranteed.

answer.text: is greater than the maximum possible number of hair on the head
is less than the maximum possible number of hair on the head
has at least one pair of identical twins

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part A Q15

questiontext.text: A metal wire is stretched along its length. Another identical wire is heated. The resultant length of the two wires is the same. What can be said about the diameters of the two wires?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Option 3 (the hot wire has a larger diameter than the stretched wire).

Stretching: wire elongates, diameter decreases (Poisson contraction). Heating: wire expands isotropically — length increases AND diameter increases. Both reach same final length. The stretched wire's diameter decreased; the heated wire's diameter increased. Hence the hot wire has a larger diameter.

answer.text: the hot wire has a larger diameter than the stretched wire
the hot wire has a smaller diameter than the stretched wire

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part A Q16

questiontext.text: The graph shows the monthly average rainfall and monthly average temperature at a certain place in India [graph omitted — refer original paper]. Where is this place most likely to be located?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Option 1 (On the west coast).

The west coast of India (Malabar) receives very heavy rainfall during June–September (SW monsoon) with moderate temperatures. The rainfall–temperature pattern shown (heavy monsoon rain, moderate temps) is characteristic of the west coast.

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part A Q17

questiontext.text: Two parallel chords of length 8 cm and 6 cm of a circle are separated by a distance of 1 cm. The radius of the circle (in cm) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Option 3 (5)

Let distances from centre to the chords be \(d_1\) and \(d_2 = d_1 + 1\). Then \(r^2 = d_1^2 + 16 = d_2^2 + 9\). So \(d_2^2 - d_1^2 = 7 \Rightarrow (d_2 - d_1)(d_2 + d_1) = 7 \Rightarrow 1\cdot(2d_1+1) = 7 \Rightarrow d_1 = 3,\; d_2 = 4\). Hence \(r^2 = 9 + 16 = 25 \Rightarrow r = 5\).

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part A Q18

questiontext.text: Graphs A and B define the same relationship between \(y\) and \(x\) for \(x, y > 0\). Graph A is a curve; Graph B is a straight line [refer original paper for figures]. The variable on the ordinate of graph B is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Option 4 (\(xy\))

If the relationship is \(y = k/x\), then \(xy = k\) = constant. Plotting \(xy\) vs \(x\) gives a horizontal straight line — a linear graph. So ordinate of the linear graph B is \(xy\).

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part A Q19

questiontext.text: The value of a physical quantity is measured to be \(3.4587 \pm 0.0022\). Which one of the following is the appropriate representation of the result taking the errors into account?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Option 3 (3.46)

The uncertainty is 0.0022, which has its first significant digit in the third decimal place. The measurement should be rounded to match: 3.4587 rounded to 2 decimal places = 3.46. So the result is \(3.46 \pm 0.002\).

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part A Q20

questiontext.text: The cross-section along two mutually perpendicular axes of a solid object are a circle and a square, respectively. The object is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Option 2 (a cylinder).

A cylinder has a circular cross-section perpendicular to its axis and a square cross-section along its axis (when height = diameter). This matches the given description.
